title: Beyond the Average: Distributional Causal Inference under Imperfect Compliance
abstract: We study the estimation of distributional treatment effects in randomized experiments with imperfect compliance. When participants do not adhere to their assigned treatments, we leverage treatment assignment as an instrumental variable to identify the local distributional treatment effect—the difference in outcome distributions between treatment and control groups for the subpopulation of compliers. We propose a regression-adjusted estimator based on a distribution regression framework with Neyman-orthogonal moment conditions, enabling robustness and flexibility with high-dimensional covariates. Our approach accommodates continuous, discrete, and mixed discrete-continuous outcomes, and applies under a broad class of covariate-adaptive randomization schemes, including stratified block designs and simple random sampling. We derive the estimator’s asymptotic distribution and show that it achieves the semiparametric efficiency bound. Simulation results demonstrate favorable finite-sample performance, and we demonstrate the method’s practical relevance in an application to the Oregon Health Insurance Experiment.

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
The paper introduces an estimator of the local distributional treatment effect (LDTE) under imperfect compliance to obtain the difference in treatment and outcome distribution for the compliers. The authors introduce a double machine learning approach to estimate the LDTE, show its theoretical properties, including asymptotic normality, and its effectiveness in practical applications.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
Strengths
-----------------
- Solid paper, introducing an important method with interesting theoretical results. In particular, I like the guarantee of uniform confidence bands.
- Proofs are well written!

Weaknesses
----------------
- While the result in Theorem 5.2. is very nice, it appears to me to not give any insights on how to actually construct confidence intervals, unless the covariance kernel can somehow be estimated. In the appendix you use bootstrap which is usually justified under asymptotic normality. Still I personally would have been more interested in a result justifying the bootstrap or another method to construct the simultaneous confidence bands, than whether beta meets the semiparametric efficiency bound.

QUESTIONS:
- The assumed convergence rates in Assumption 5.1 (i) appear relatively strong in the context of the usual assumption in double-machine learning (where thanks to orthogonalization weaker rates can be assumed usually). Is this just unavoidable in this context?
- Just to clarify, you explain that Assumption 3.1 (i) allows for corss-sectional dependence among treatment statuses. This is because Z_i is left out of the i.i.d. assumption? 
- In Equation (2), if I understand correctly, you basically have a ratio of two AIPW estimators. How does this affect the theoretical handling of the estimator as opposed to the usual AIPW estimator? Is this a reason for the convergence rate assumptions?
- I would add () around the citations in line 152

LIMITATIONS:
Yes
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
This paper introduces an estimator for the local distributional treatment effect, which is an instrumental-variable-type estimator for the causal effect among compliers. They prove asymptotic properties of the estimator and empirically argue using synthetic and real data that the estimator is more effective than baselines.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
Strengths:
- while I do have some confusions outlined below, I think the contribution here is probably theoretically solid and well-scoped
- experiments are mostly clear and compelling

Weaknesses:
- could use some more clarity on exactly what the contribution here is - is it just adding a distributional component onto existing IV estimators for complier effects? or something further. I think it's more but have trouble totally figuring it out, the distributional component by itself seems like probably not quite enough novelty but I could be wrong. Lemma 3.2 would be a nice place to do some of this clarification but anywhere is fine
- confused about a couple fundamental pieces here: the authors define \eta_z = E[D | Z, S, X] on L193. However according to the causal graph in Fig 1 it looks like D should be independent of X, S given Z - why is this not just E[D | Z] in that case? Similarly I wonder about the equation below on L194 - I would assume from Fig 1 that Y should be independent of X, S given Z
- Algo 1: should clarify what exactly M is trained on, inputs + outputs. I don't see this notation elsewhere
- Assumption 5.1: (I) I find it confusing why we need a condition on the difference but not on the individual components, (ii) the < \infty part of this is confusing to me as well, doesn't this hold trivially for any F bounded above by 1? which \mu_z should be since it is an Expectation of an indicator function
- Theorem 5.2 - I may just not have the theory background here so apologies but I don't really understand what it means for this to converge to a GP. Don't we want it to be decreasing in some function of n?
- Fig 4: your left and right plots look identical to me, I'm assuming this is an error

QUESTIONS:
- clarify the contribution beyond previous work 
- correct Fig 4 so that the L and R parts are different
- clarify how I should interpret causal graph in Fig 1 and how it interacts with the definitions of \eta and \mu

LIMITATIONS:
n/a
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
This paper proposes a framework for estimating the local distributional treatment effect for experiments with non-compliance for covariate-dependent randomization scenarios. For that, they provide a distributional regression-adjusted estimator. The authors derive the asymptotic properties of the estimator and show that, given additional assumptions, it can achieve the semi-parametric efficiency bound.  Then they provide results for experiments on synthetic and real-world data.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
# Strengths:
- Extending the LDTE for the CAR setting with non-compliance seems to be novel and is of real-world relevance since non-compliance exists in various applications from A/B tests in marketing to clinical trials in medicine.
- The paper provides proper theoretical proofs for most of its statements in the main paper and in the Appendix.
- Providing insights on real-world data with the Oregon Health Insurance Experiment is a suitable application for their method and goes beyond only synthetic evaluations as often seen in causal inference research.
# Weaknesses:
- Some parts of the paper are only described vaguely, not sufficiently supported by additional evidence, or are hard to understand. Examples in the following:
    -  ll.205: Here, the first-order sensitivity of the proposed estimator should be shown
    - ll: 208: “To further improve robustness, we incorporate cross-fitting with L folds (L > 1) as proposed by Chernozhukov et al. (2018).” I think this really vague. The cross-fitting is necessary to ensure the theoretical guarantees of the estimators, which should be listed above.
    - The authors state that their method is “applicable to continuous, discrete, and mixed discrete-continuous outcomes”. However, in Algorithm 1, it is necessary to iterate through all levels of $\mathcal{Y}$, which should be infeasible for continuous outcomes, or requires strong simplifications such as stratifications for which guaranteeing identifiability is not always straightforward. Overall, since this statement also appears in the abstract, I think evidence for that should be provided.
- The empirical evaluation could be improved clearly. Here, only ML adjustment and linear adjustments are compared. No baselines are added (e.g., comparing it to a naive ITT estimate of the DTE, etc.). If none are applicable or not useful, I would strongly suggest discussing this in the paper. Also, for the synthetic data, what values for $y$ are evaluated?
- The contribution of the method is limited, as it mostly seems to extend Abadie, A. (2002) to the CAR setting.

Minor:
- I think the challenges of CAR and the connection to regression adjustment could be described more clearly in the introduction to give a better intuition of their contribution extending over simple randomization.
- All equations should be numbered.
- Plot descriptions should be standalone and better explain the plots and their implications.

QUESTIONS:
- l.56: What is meant by “traditional i.i.d. framework” in this context? Does this refer to normal randomized treatment assignment independent of covariates?
- The equation after l.127 holds only under consistency assumptions for potential outcomes of Y and potential outcomes of D, right? I think this should be stated and maybe, together with the formal description of the exclusion assumption, added to assumptions 3.1
- Eq. (1): The notation here (and later on) seems to me a bit untypical. Is there any reason to express the CDFs here as expectations over indicators instead of using the probability as after l.164? Also, in the denominator, for binary D, I would usually expect $\mathbb{P}(D=1|...)$ instead of $\mathbb{E}[D|...]$
- l. 289: What is the Local Probability Treatment Effect, and why is it only introduced here for the evaluation?

LIMITATIONS:
Major limitations are mentioned in the paper.
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
In this paper, the author studies the problem of distributional effect estimation under imperfect compliance. In the imperfect compliance setting, people can choose to move to different treatments or not comply with the treatment they were assigned. While there have been many works on treatment effect estimation under imperfect compliance, there is little known work on distributional effect estimation, which measures the distributional effect between treatment and control groups. In this paper, the authors introduce a regression-adjusted estimator for local distributional treatment effects (LDTEs) with covariate adaptive randomization (CAR). They provide asymptotic properties, including semi-parametric efficiency, and demonstrate the effectiveness of the estimator on real and synthetic data sets. The real data set is the Oregon Health Insurance Experiment, where compliance is only 50-60%.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
**Strengths**

The authors address an important question of distributional effect estimation (quantile treatment effects). While most work has focused on mean estimation, there has been a growing body of work for the more challenging distributional estimation estimation problem. This work contributes to that body, under an important realistic setting of imperfect compliance.

The paper is the first to combine regression adjustment and instrumental variable methods for estimating full outcome distributions under CAR and imperfect compliance.

**Weaknesses**

It is not entirely clear why a strata based conditional estimator was proposed. The authors could have easily studied the same setting without introducing strata, and just using covariates. It would also be interesting to see if there is any empirical (or theoretical) performance gains obtained by stratifying.

It is also not clear from the paper, why balancing based approaches fail for distributional effect estimation (among the complied). The authors propose a regression based estimator.

QUESTIONS:
Can the authors comment on **non-asymptotic** properties of the regression estimator proposed? How exactly would it depend on sampling strategy.

LIMITATIONS:
N/A
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
DECISION:
Accept (poster)

COMMENT:
The authors propose a new approach for estimating distributional (quantile) treatment effects in randomized experiments with covariate-adaptive randomization (CAR), which allows treatment assignment to be correlated across units, and noncompliance (e.g., participants do not adhere to their assigned treatment).  While distributional treatment effects and CAR have been studied separately, this is the first work to integrate these areas of causal inference.  The authors propose a doubly robust, sample-efficient regression-adjusted estimator for the local distributional treatment effect (for compliers, across treatment arms).  They prove a semiparametric efficiency bound for this estimator and show theoretically and empirically that the variance is lower for the regression-adjusted as compared to an unadjusted estimator.  Finite-sample performance is evaluated for a simulation study and for real-world data from the Oregon Health Insurance Experiment.

Strengths:
* Both distributional treatment effects and noncompliance are important areas of causal inference, and this paper integrates the two in a rigorous, correct, and compelling way.
* The authors provide convincing evidence (theory, simulation, and an experiment on real-world data) that the regression-adjusted estimator outperforms the unadjusted estimator.
* The paper is generally clear and well written.
* Theoretical results are interesting, and experiments are clear and compelling.  I particularly appreciated the use of real-world Oregon Health Insurance Experiment data rather than just simulations.

Weaknesses:
* The main theoretical results are not particularly surprising - e.g., the proposed estimator can be written as the ratio of two augmented inverse probability weighted (AIPW) estimates for change in outcome distribution and change in treatment uptake respectively, and one might expect covariate adjustment to improve statistical efficiency and reduce variance. (Nevertheless, the results are useful and derived in a rigorous and convincing manner.)
* Various aspects of the original submission needed clarification, though these were resolved adequately in discussion.

Reason for accept/reject decision: This is a solid paper which makes a useful and important contribution to the causal inference ML literature, addressing the combination of distributional treatment effects and CAR/noncompliance for the first time.  I believe the paper should be accepted, but would hesitate to award a spotlight or oral given that the results are strong and useful but not earth-shattering.

Discussion: There was a robust discussion between the authors and reviewers, in which the authors were able to clarify several points which were initially unclear to the reviewers, for example (i) making it clear that the current work incorporates CAR while prior work of Abadie (2002) assumes i.i.d. treatment assignment; (ii) defining the local probability treatment effect; (iii) clarifying specific aspects of the model and assumptions; and (iv) explaining the convergence rate. It is important for these additional discussion points to be incorporated into the final version of the paper, and I believe that we can rely on the authors to do so, particularly given that this will further increase the accessibility and impact of the paper. No major issues, nor need for substantial additional experiments, were identified during the discussion, and the reviewers were generally satisfied with the authors' responses, rating the paper between borderline accept and full accept after discussion.
reviews.TITLE: Decision
